Parallel Generation Scheme (PGS):
Initialize the clock time (t = 0), the set of scheduled activities (S = {0}), and the set of eligible activities (E) that have all predecessors in S.
Identify the set of decision activities (D) from the eligible set (E) whose resource requirements can be met by the currently available resource capacities at time t.
Select an activity from the decision set (D) based on a predefined priority rule (e.g., Earliest Finish Time, Minimum Slack, or Greatest Resource Demand).
Schedule the selected activity to start at time t, update the remaining resource capacities for the duration of the activity, and move it from E to the set of in-progress activities (P).
Repeat the selection and scheduling process until no more activities in the decision set (D) can be scheduled at the current clock time t.
Advance the clock time (t) to the earliest completion time of all activities currently in the in-progress set (P).
Update the set of scheduled activities (S) by adding the completed activities, release their resources back to the available pool, and update the eligible set (E) with new activities whose predecessors are now all in S.
Repeat the process of advancing time and scheduling activities until all activities in the project, including the dummy sink node, have been scheduled.
Minimum Slack Priority Rule (MSL):
Initialize the project clock at time t = 0 and define the set of completed activities as empty.
Identify the set of activities that have no predecessors or whose predecessors have all been completed.
Calculate the Earliest Start Time (EST) and Earliest Finish Time (EFT) for all activities using a forward pass through the project network.
Calculate the Latest Start Time (LST) and Latest Finish Time (LFT) for all activities using a backward pass based on the project deadline or critical path length.
Calculate the static slack for each activity using the formula: Slack = LST - EST.
Identify the 'Eligible Set' of activities that are precedence-feasible (all predecessors are finished) but have not yet started.
Sort the activities in the Eligible Set in ascending order of their slack values; activities with the minimum slack receive the highest priority.
Check the resource availability for the activity with the highest priority (lowest slack) at the current time t.
If resources are sufficient, schedule the activity to start at time t, update the remaining resource capacity, and remove it from the Eligible Set.
If resources are insufficient, skip the activity and attempt to schedule the next activity in the priority list.
Advance the project clock t to the earliest completion time of currently running activities once no more activities can be started at the current time.
Update the set of completed activities and the Eligible Set based on the new project clock and finished tasks.
Repeat the prioritization and scheduling process until all activities in the project have been assigned a start and finish time.
Greatest Resource Demand Priority Rule (:
Initialize the project data including the set of activities, their durations, precedence relationships, and resource requirements.
Set the current simulation time to zero and initialize the set of completed activities as empty.
Calculate the Resource Demand for each activity by multiplying its duration by the sum of its required resource units across all resource types.
Identify the set of 'eligible' activities that have all their predecessors completed and have not yet started.
Sort the eligible activities in descending order based on their calculated Resource Demand (GRD value).
Check the availability of renewable resources for the highest-priority activity in the sorted eligible list.
If resources are sufficient, schedule the activity to start at the current time, update resource availability, and move it to the 'in-progress' set.
If resources are insufficient, skip the activity and attempt to schedule the next activity in the priority list.
Advance the simulation time to the earliest completion time of all currently running activities.
Update the set of completed activities, release the resources used by finished activities, and update the eligible set.
Repeat the prioritization and scheduling process until all activities have been assigned a start and finish time.
Output the final schedule and calculate the project makespan.
Most Successors Priority Rule (MSN):
Define the project data including the set of activities, durations, resource requirements, resource capacities, and the precedence relationship graph.
Initialize the set of scheduled activities as empty, the set of eligible activities (those whose predecessors are all completed) with the project start node, and the current clock time at zero.
Calculate the priority value for each activity by counting the total number of direct and indirect successors in the precedence graph.
Identify the 'Eligible Set' of activities that have not yet been scheduled and whose predecessors have finished by the current decision point.
Sort the activities in the Eligible Set in descending order based on their number of successors (MSN rule).
In case of a tie in the number of successors, apply a secondary tie-breaking rule such as 'Minimum Activity Duration' or 'Smallest Activity ID'.
Select the activity with the highest priority from the sorted Eligible Set and check if the required resources are available for its entire duration.
If resources are available, schedule the activity to start at the current time, update the resource profile, and remove it from the Eligible Set.
If resources are not available, skip to the next activity in the priority list or increment the clock time to the next earliest activity completion point.
Update the Eligible Set based on the newly completed activities and repeat the selection and scheduling process until all activities are assigned a start time.
Calculate the project makespan based on the finish time of the final project completion node.
Latest Start Time Priority Rule (LST):
Calculate the Minimum Project Duration by performing a Forward Pass to determine the Early Start (ES) and Early Finish (EF) times for each activity.
Perform a Backward Pass starting from the project deadline (or minimum duration) to calculate the Latest Finish (LF) and Latest Start (LS) times for all activities.
Initialize the scheduling clock at time t = 0 and define the set of 'Eligible Activities' whose predecessors have all been completed.
Identify the 'Ready Set' of activities from the eligible pool that can start at the current time t without violating resource constraints.
Sort the activities in the Ready Set in ascending order based on their Latest Start (LS) times (the LST priority rule).
Select the activity with the smallest LS value and check if current available resource capacities are sufficient to meet its requirements.
If resources are sufficient, schedule the activity to start at time t, update the remaining resource capacity, and remove it from the eligible pool.
If resources are insufficient for the highest priority activity, skip it and attempt to schedule the next activity in the sorted priority list.
Advance the scheduling clock to the next earliest completion time of an active activity once no more activities can be scheduled at time t.
Update the set of Eligible Activities based on the newly completed tasks and repeat the selection and scheduling process until all activities are assigned a start time.
Latest Finish Time Priority Rule (LFT):
Perform a Forward Pass through the project network to calculate the Earliest Start (ES) and Earliest Finish (EF) times for each activity, determining the minimum project duration.
Perform a Backward Pass starting from the project deadline (or minimum duration) to calculate the Latest Finish Time (LFT) and Latest Start (LS) for each activity.
Initialize the scheduling clock (t = 0), the set of completed activities, and the set of available resources.
Identify the 'Eligible Set' of activities whose predecessors have all been completed.
Sort the activities in the Eligible Set in ascending order based on their Latest Finish Time (LFT); activities with the smallest LFT receive the highest priority.
In case of a tie in LFT, apply a secondary tie-breaking rule, such as Minimum Slack or Shortest Processing Time.
Iterate through the prioritized Eligible Set and check if the required resources for the activity are available at the current time t.
If resources are available, schedule the activity to start at time t, update the resource profile for the duration of the activity, and move it to the 'In-Progress' set.
If resources are insufficient, skip the activity and check the next one in the prioritized list.
Advance the clock t to the next earliest completion time of an activity currently in progress.
Update the sets of completed and eligible activities based on the new time t and release the resources used by finished activities.
Repeat the selection and scheduling process until all activities have been assigned a start and finish time.
Earliest Start Time Priority Rule (EST):
Define the project data, including the set of activities, their durations, precedence relationships, and resource requirements, as well as the total available resource capacities.
Initialize the set of completed activities as empty, the set of eligible activities (those whose predecessors are finished) with the project start dummy activity, and set the current project clock to zero.
Maintain a list of 'scheduled' activities with their assigned start and finish times and a 'resource profile' tracking resource usage over time.
Identify all 'eligible' activities from the precedence graph where all immediate predecessors have already been completed.
Apply the Earliest Start Time (EST) priority rule by calculating the earliest possible start time for each eligible activity based on the finish times of its predecessors.
Sort the eligible activities in ascending order of their EST values; in case of ties, use a secondary rule like minimum duration or lowest activity index.
Select the activity with the lowest EST and check if the required resources are available during the interval from its EST to its estimated finish time.
If resources are available, schedule the activity to start at its EST, update the resource profile, and move the activity from the eligible set to the scheduled set.
If resources are not available at the EST, delay the activity's start time to the earliest point in time when both precedence constraints and resource constraints are satisfied.
Update the set of eligible activities by checking which unscheduled activities now have all their predecessors completed.
Repeat the selection and scheduling process until all activities, including the project end dummy activity, have been assigned a start and finish time.
Output the final schedule, including the start times for all activities and the total project makespan.
Earliest Finish Time Priority Rule (EFT):
Initialize the set of scheduled activities with the dummy start activity at time zero.
Identify the set of eligible activities whose predecessors have all been completed.
Calculate the Earliest Finish Time (EFT) for each eligible activity, defined as the earliest possible start time (based on predecessor completion) plus its duration.
Sort the eligible activities in ascending order of their Earliest Finish Times.
Select the activity with the smallest EFT from the sorted list.
Check the availability of required renewable resources for the selected activity starting from its earliest possible start time.
If resources are insufficient, delay the start time to the earliest point where both precedence and resource constraints are satisfied, then recalculate its finish time.
Schedule the activity, update the resource profile over its duration, and add it to the set of completed activities.
Update the set of eligible activities by checking which unscheduled activities now have all predecessors completed.
Repeat the selection and scheduling process until all activities, including the dummy sink activity, have been assigned a finish time.
Shortest Processing Time Priority Rule (:
Identify all project data including the set of activities, their durations, resource requirements, and precedence constraints.
Initialize the project clock at time t = 0 and define the set of completed activities as empty.
Identify the set of 'eligible' activities whose predecessors have all been completed.
Sort the eligible activities in non-decreasing order based on their processing times (durations).
Select the activity with the shortest processing time from the sorted eligible list.
Check if the required resources for the selected activity are available at the current time t.
If resources are available, schedule the activity to start at time t and subtract the required resources from the available pool.
If resources are not available, skip to the next activity in the sorted eligible list and repeat the resource check.
Update the project clock to the next earliest completion time of an ongoing activity.
Release the resources held by the completed activity and add it to the set of completed activities.
Update the set of eligible activities based on the newly completed activity.
Repeat the selection and scheduling process until all activities have been assigned a start and finish time.
Longest Processing Time Priority Rule (L:
Define the project data including the set of activities, durations, precedence relationships, and resource constraints.
Initialize the current clock time to zero and the set of available resources to their maximum capacities.
Identify the set of activities that have no predecessors or whose predecessors have already been completed.
Sort the set of eligible activities in descending order based on their processing times (durations).
Select the activity with the longest processing time from the sorted list of eligible activities.
Check if the required resources for the selected activity are available at the current clock time.
If resources are available, schedule the activity to start at the current clock time and update the resource availability for the duration of the activity.
If resources are not available, move to the next activity in the sorted list and repeat the resource check.
Update the current clock time to the earliest completion time of the currently running activities.
Release the resources used by completed activities and update the set of eligible activities based on new precedence satisfactions.
Repeat the sorting, selection, and scheduling process until all activities in the project have been scheduled.
Calculate the total project makespan based on the finish time of the final activity.
